Algebraic curves higher genus

Genus $g \ge 2$: Riemann–Roch, the canonical embedding, and $\mathcal{M}_g$.

blocks.html:
1. Smooth projective curves and their genus

A smooth projective curve over an algebraically closed field $k$ is a smooth projective integral $k$-scheme of dimension $1$. Concretely: a one-dimensional scheme that locally looks like $\mathrm{Spec}\, A$ with $A$ a regular ring of Krull dimension $1$, no embedded points, no nodes, no cusps. Over $k = \mathbb{C}$ the GAGA equivalence identifies smooth projective curves with compact Riemann surfaces — the same data wears two coats, algebraic and analytic.

The single most important invariant is the genus $g = \dim_k H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$, the dimension of the space of regular (= holomorphic) $1$-forms. Three other quantities give the same number, by deep theorems each worth its own name: the topological genus of $C(\mathbb{C})$ (number of handles), the arithmetic genus $1 - \chi(\mathcal{O}_C) = 1 - h^0(\mathcal{O}_C) + h^1(\mathcal{O}_C) = h^1(\mathcal{O}_C)$, and $1 - \tfrac12\chi_{\mathrm{top}}$. That all four agree on a smooth projective curve is the curve case of the Hirzebruch–Riemann–Roch theorem; in higher dimensions arithmetic and geometric genera diverge.

Genus partitions curves into ladders of qualitatively different worlds. Genus $0$ is rigid: the unique smooth projective curve of genus $0$ over $\bar k$ is $\mathbb{P}^1$; there is no moduli. Genus $1$ is the territory of elliptic curves — a one-parameter family classified by the $j$-invariant, every such curve a complex torus $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$. From genus $2$ onward the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$ has dimension $3g - 3$ and the geometry stratifies further: hyperelliptic vs. non-hyperelliptic, generic vs. special Weierstrass points, $\theta$-divisor pathologies. Everything below is the catalogue of the new structure that appears once we cross the $g \ge 2$ threshold.

The numerology is rigid enough to be worth memorizing in advance:

quantityvaluecharacter
genus $g$$g \ge 0$topological / analytic / algebraic — all agree
$\chi_{\mathrm{top}}$$2 - 2g$topological
$\dim H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$$g$Hodge
$\deg K_C$$2g - 2$algebro-geometric (canonical divisor)
$\dim \mathcal{M}_g$ ($g \ge 2$)$3g - 3$moduli-theoretic

<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#svg-rh-cover');
  const out = $('#out-rh-cover');
  const dEl = $('#rh-d'), bEl = $('#rh-b');
  const dValEl = $('#rh-d-val'), bValEl = $('#rh-b-val');

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    const d = +dEl.value, b = +bEl.value;
    dValEl.textContent = 'd = ' + d;
    bValEl.textContent = 'b = ' + b;

    // upper line: P^1 base with branch points marked
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:18,y:208,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--mute)'}));
    const baseY = 200;
    const baseLine = SVG('line',{x1:60,y1:baseY,x2:480,y2:baseY,stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':2});
    svg.appendChild(baseLine);
    const tBase = SVG('text',{x:495,y:baseY+4,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--blue)'});
    tBase.textContent = 'ℙ¹';
    svg.appendChild(tBase);

    // branch points evenly spaced on base
    const branches = [];
    for(let i=0;i<b;i++){
      const x = 90 + (i+1) * (380 / (b+1));
      branches.push(x);
      const c = SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:baseY,r:5,fill:'var(--pink)','stroke':'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1});
      c.style.cursor = 'pointer';
      c.dataset.idx = i;
      svg.appendChild(c);
    }

    // cover sheets — d horizontal slabs above base, with merging dots over branch points
    const topY = 50, sheetGap = 22;
    for(let s=0;s<d;s++){
      const y = topY + s*sheetGap;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:60,y1:y,x2:480,y2:y,stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':1.4,opacity:0.85}));
    }
    const tCov = SVG('text',{x:495,y:topY+4,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--yellow)'});
    tCov.textContent = 'C';
    svg.appendChild(tCov);

    // mark fibres above each branch point: simple branch ⇒ two sheets meet, others stay separate
    branches.forEach((x,i)=>{
      // ramification dot at top
      const ramY = topY + 0.5*sheetGap;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:ramY,r:4,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
      // remaining d-2 sheets just have ordinary dots
      for(let s=2;s<d;s++){
        svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:topY+s*sheetGap,r:2.5,fill:'var(--yellow)'}));
      }
      // dashed projection arrow
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:x,y1:topY+(d-1)*sheetGap+6,x2:x,y2:baseY-7,stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1,'stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
    });

    // legend at left
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:10,y:topY+8,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'})).textContent = 'C';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:10,y:baseY+5,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'})).textContent = 'P¹';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:10,y:(topY+baseY)/2,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)','font-style':'italic'})).textContent = 'π';

    // genus from Riemann–Hurwitz: 2g_C - 2 = d(2 g_{P^1} - 2) + sum_p (e_p - 1).
    // base is P^1 (g=0), each simple branch contributes (2-1) = 1 to ramification, with d-2 unramified sheets above.
    // So: 2g - 2 = d(-2) + b ⇒ g = (b - 2d + 2)/2 = b/2 - d + 1.
    const g = (b - 2*d + 2)/2;
    let line1 = `degree d = ${d},  branch points b = ${b}`;
    let line2 = `Riemann–Hurwitz:  2g − 2 = d(2·0 − 2) + b`;
    let line3 = `              = −2d + b = ${-2*d + b}`;
    let line4 = `cover genus  g = (b − 2d + 2)/2 = ${g}`;
    if(g < 0) line4 += '   ✗ impossible (need b ≥ 2d − 2)';
    out.textContent = [line1, line2, line3, line4].join('\n');
  }

  dEl.addEventListener('input', draw);
  bEl.addEventListener('input', draw);
  // click a branch point: increment b by 2 (must stay even-ish for simple branching with no extra constraint),
  // or decrement on shift-click
  svg.addEventListener('click', e=>{
    const t = e.target;
    if(t && t.tagName==='circle' && t.dataset.idx!==undefined){
      const cur = +bEl.value;
      const next = e.shiftKey ? Math.max(0, cur-2) : Math.min(10, cur+2);
      bEl.value = next;
      draw();
    }
  });
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: The widget evaluates the Riemann–Hurwitz formula: for a degree-$d$ cover $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ with simple branching at $b$ points, the cover's genus is $g = b/2 - d + 1$. Two visceral consequences: (i) covers of $\mathbb{P}^1$ realise every genus $g \ge 0$ as soon as $d \ge 2$ and $b = 2g + 2d - 2$; (ii) requiring $g \ge 0$ forces $b \ge 2d - 2$, the bottom of the moduli stratification. The hyperelliptic case ($d = 2$, $b = 2g+2$) is the leftmost edge of this picture and reappears in §5.

2. Divisors on curves

A divisor on a smooth projective curve $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb{Z}$-sum $D = \sum_p n_p [p]$ over closed points. Divisors form a free abelian group $\mathrm{Div}(C)$; the degree map $\deg\colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb{Z}$, $\deg D = \sum n_p \deg(p/k)$, is a homomorphism. The principal divisors $\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_p \mathrm{ord}_p(f) \cdot [p]$ for $f \in k(C)^\times$ form a subgroup of degree $0$, and the Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \mathrm{Div}(C)/\mathrm{Princ}(C)$ classifies isomorphism classes of line bundles on $C$ — equivalently, divisors up to linear equivalence.

The degree map descends to $\deg\colon \mathrm{Pic}(C) \to \mathbb{Z}$, splitting the Picard group into degree slices: $\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \bigsqcup_d \mathrm{Pic}^d(C)$. Each slice is a torsor over the kernel $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$, the Jacobian. Over $\mathbb{C}$ a stunning fact: $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C) \cong \mathbb{C}^g / \Lambda_C$, a $g$-dimensional complex torus, where $\Lambda_C \subset \mathbb{C}^g$ is the period lattice of integrals of holomorphic $1$-forms over a basis of $H_1(C, \mathbb{Z}) \cong \mathbb{Z}^{2g}$. For $g = 1$ this lattice is exactly the lattice of $\mathbb{C}/\Lambda$ from the elliptic-curves topic; the elliptic curve is its own Jacobian, the only genus where this happens.

For $g \ge 2$ the Jacobian acquires extra structure: a principal polarization (the $\theta$-divisor), an Abel–Jacobi map $\mathrm{AJ}\colon C \to \mathrm{Jac}(C)$, $p \mapsto \int_{p_0}^p \omega$, and via the symmetric powers $\mathrm{Sym}^d C \to \mathrm{Pic}^d(C)$ a description of effective divisors as fibres of a proper map. The widget below is a 2D slice of the period lattice — a faithful picture only for $g = 1$, but a useful ladder for visualising what "complex torus of dimension $g$" means.

3. Riemann–Roch on curves

For a divisor $D$ on a smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g$, the Riemann–Roch theorem equates two dimension counts: $$h^0(D) - h^0(K - D) = \deg D - g + 1,$$ where $K = K_C$ is the canonical divisor (any divisor of a regular $1$-form $\omega \in H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$). The first term $h^0(D) = \dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal{O}_C(D))$ counts global sections of $\mathcal{O}_C(D)$ — equivalently, rational functions $f$ with $\mathrm{div}(f) + D \ge 0$. The second term, $h^0(K - D) = h^1(D)$ by Serre duality, is the obstruction term.

The reason genus controls everything: Riemann–Roch is exactly the statement $\chi(\mathcal{O}_C(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$, that is, the Euler characteristic of any line bundle is degree-shifted-by-genus. Three immediate consequences are worth keeping at the front of the mind:

  • Vanishing. If $\deg D > 2g - 2$, then $\deg(K - D) < 0$ so $h^0(K - D) = 0$ and Riemann–Roch becomes the equality $h^0(D) = \deg D - g + 1$.
  • Canonical bundle. Setting $D = K$ gives $h^0(K) - h^0(0) = (2g - 2) - g + 1$, i.e. $h^0(K) = g$ — recovering the definition of genus.
  • Inequality. Always $h^0(D) \ge \deg D - g + 1$, useful for proving sections exist when degrees are large.

blocks.html: Worked example: for $D = nq$ with $q \in C$ a fixed point on a genus-$g$ curve, the dimensions $h^0(nq)$ as $n$ grows are determined by the Weierstrass gap sequence: there are exactly $g$ "gap" values $1 \le n_1 < \cdots < n_g \le 2g - 1$ where $h^0(nq) = h^0((n-1)q)$, and at all other $n$ the dimension goes up by $1$. For a generic point $q$ the gaps are $\{1, 2, \ldots, g\}$; for a Weierstrass point they jump (e.g. on a hyperelliptic curve the gaps at a Weierstrass point are $\{1, 3, 5, \ldots, 2g-1\}$). The worked $g = 2$, $D = 3[p]$ case in the scrubber above puts us at $n = 3 = 2g - 1$, the last possible gap value.

4. The canonical embedding

On a curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 2$ the canonical linear system $|K|$ is base-point-free (Riemann–Roch + a short argument), and it has dimension $g - 1$ since $h^0(K) = g$. Choose a basis $\omega_1, \ldots, \omega_g$ of $H^0(C, \Omega^1_C)$. The canonical map $$\varphi_K\colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{g-1}, \qquad p \longmapsto [\omega_1(p) : \cdots : \omega_g(p)]$$ is a morphism whose image is intrinsic to $C$ up to projective isomorphism — different basis choices differ by elements of $\mathrm{PGL}_g$.

Max Noether's theorem (or "Enriques–Babbage") says: $\varphi_K$ is an embedding if and only if $C$ is non-hyperelliptic, in which case the image is a smooth projective curve of degree $\deg K = 2g - 2$ in $\mathbb{P}^{g-1}$. When $C$ is hyperelliptic, $\varphi_K$ is the composition $C \xrightarrow{2:1} \mathbb{P}^1 \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{g-1}$ where the second map is the rational normal curve of degree $g - 1$; the canonical map factors through the hyperelliptic involution and is no longer injective.

The canonical model is the most natural way to put an abstract curve into projective space: it is functorial in the sense that any automorphism of $C$ extends to a projective automorphism of the canonical image. For low genus the canonical image is concrete:

  • $g = 2$: $\varphi_K\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ is the unique $g^1_2$ — every genus-$2$ curve is hyperelliptic, no non-hyperelliptic case exists.
  • $g = 3$: non-hyperelliptic ⇒ $\varphi_K(C)$ is a smooth quartic in $\mathbb{P}^2$ ($\deg = 4$, $\dim = 1$); hyperelliptic ⇒ image is a smooth conic $= \mathbb{P}^1$ in $\mathbb{P}^2$, mapped $2:1$.
  • $g = 4$: non-hyperelliptic ⇒ $\varphi_K(C) \subset \mathbb{P}^3$ is the intersection of a unique quadric and a cubic (Petri's theorem in low genus).
  • $g \ge 4$ generic: the canonical image is the intersection of all the quadrics through it (Noether–Enriques–Petri).

  • Kähler differentials $\Omega^1_{X/k}$

5. Hyperelliptic curves

A smooth projective curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 2$ is hyperelliptic if it admits a degree-$2$ map $\pi\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^1$. Equivalently — by Riemann–Hurwitz with $d = 2$ and $b = 2g + 2$ branch points, each with simple ramification — every hyperelliptic curve is birational to an affine model $$y^2 = f(x), \qquad f(x) \in k[x] \text{ separable of degree } 2g+1 \text{ or } 2g+2.$$ The map $\pi$ is the projection $(x, y) \mapsto x$, the involution $\sigma\colon (x, y) \mapsto (x, -y)$ (the "hyperelliptic involution") generates $\mathrm{Gal}(C/\mathbb{P}^1) = \mathbb{Z}/2$, and the $2g+2$ branch points are the roots of $f$ together with $\infty$ (if $\deg f$ is odd).

Three different characterisations agree, and switching between them is the recurring move:

  • Geometric: $\exists$ a degree-$2$ map $C \to \mathbb{P}^1$ — equivalently, $C$ carries a $g^1_2$ (a $1$-dimensional linear system of degree $2$).
  • Canonical: the canonical map $\varphi_K\colon C \to \mathbb{P}^{g-1}$ is not an embedding — it factors through $\mathbb{P}^1$.
  • Riemann–Roch: there exist two distinct points $p, q$ with $h^0(p + q) = 2$, i.e. $|p + q|$ separates points up to the hyperelliptic involution.

Every genus-$2$ curve is hyperelliptic — its canonical map is exactly the unique $g^1_2$. From genus $3$ onward "generic" curves are non-hyperelliptic; hyperellipticity is a closed codimension-$(g-2)$ condition in $\mathcal{M}_g$. So in $\mathcal{M}_3$ hyperelliptic curves form a divisor (codim $1$); in $\mathcal{M}_4$ they form a codim-$2$ surface; in higher genus they are increasingly special.

click a branch point — see the involution swap
g = 3 → 2g+2 = 8 branch points
params.bodyScript:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#svg-hyper-cover');
  const out = $('#out-hyper-cover');
  const gEl = $('#hyper-g'), gValEl = $('#hyper-g-val');
  let selected = -1;

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    const g = +gEl.value;
    const b = 2*g + 2;
    gValEl.textContent = `g = ${g} → 2g+2 = ${b} branch points`;

    const baseY = 220, topY = 60;
    // base P^1
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:50,y1:baseY,x2:500,y2:baseY,stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':2}));
    const tBase = SVG('text',{x:512,y:baseY+4,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--blue)'}); tBase.textContent='ℙ¹'; svg.appendChild(tBase);
    // upper hyperelliptic curve sketch — two sheets joining at branch points
    // top sheet (y = +sqrt) and bottom sheet (y = −sqrt) drawn as wavy paths above base
    let topPath = `M50 ${topY+30}`;
    let botPath = `M50 ${topY+30}`;
    const xs = [];
    for(let i=0;i<b;i++){
      const x = 70 + (i+1)*(420/(b+1));
      xs.push(x);
    }
    // produce two sheets that meet at each x in xs (cycle around)
    let lastX = 50;
    let above = 1;  // upper or lower starts
    xs.forEach((x,i)=>{
      const midX = (lastX + x)/2;
      // top arc: from (lastX, topY+30) up to (x, topY+30), curving upward
      topPath += ` Q ${midX} ${topY+30 - 35} ${x} ${topY+30}`;
      botPath += ` Q ${midX} ${topY+30 + 35} ${x} ${topY+30}`;
      lastX = x;
    });
    topPath += ` Q ${(lastX+500)/2} ${topY+30 - 35} 500 ${topY+30}`;
    botPath += ` Q ${(lastX+500)/2} ${topY+30 + 35} 500 ${topY+30}`;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:topPath,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':1.6}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:botPath,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--yellow)','stroke-width':1.6}));
    const tCov = SVG('text',{x:512,y:topY+34,'font-size':12,fill:'var(--yellow)'}); tCov.textContent='C'; svg.appendChild(tCov);

    // branch points on base
    xs.forEach((x,i)=>{
      const c = SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:baseY,r:5,fill:'var(--pink)','stroke':'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1});
      c.style.cursor='pointer';
      c.dataset.idx = i;
      svg.appendChild(c);
      // and a single point on C above (sheets meet)
      const cTop = SVG('circle',{cx:x,cy:topY+30,r:5,fill:i===selected?'var(--violet)':'var(--pink)','stroke':'var(--ink)','stroke-width':1});
      cTop.style.cursor='pointer';
      cTop.dataset.idx = i;
      svg.appendChild(cTop);
      // dashed projection
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:x,y1:topY+38,x2:x,y2:baseY-7,stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1,'stroke-dasharray':'3 3'}));
    });

    // hyperelliptic involution arrows over a non-branch fibre (right-most, at x ≈ 480)
    const probeX = 480;
    const yHi = topY+30 - 25, yLo = topY+30 + 25;
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:probeX,cy:yHi,r:4,fill:'var(--cyan)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:probeX,cy:yLo,r:4,fill:'var(--cyan)'}));
    drawArrow(svg,[probeX-2,yHi+3],[probeX-2,yLo-3],{color:'var(--cyan)',markerId:'hyper-arr',pad1:6,pad2:6,curve:14,label:'σ',labelSide:0});
    drawArrow(svg,[probeX+2,yLo-3],[probeX+2,yHi+3],{color:'var(--cyan)',markerId:'hyper-arr',pad1:6,pad2:6,curve:-14});

    // legend
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:14,y:topY+34,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'})).textContent = 'C';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:14,y:baseY+5,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)'})).textContent = 'ℙ¹';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:14,y:(topY+baseY+30)/2,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--mute)','font-style':'italic'})).textContent = 'π (deg 2)';
    svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:probeX-100,y:yHi-12,'font-size':11,fill:'var(--cyan)'})).textContent = 'generic fibre: 2 points swapped by σ';

    // readout
    let lines = [];
    lines.push(`genus g = ${g}`);
    lines.push(`Riemann–Hurwitz: 2g − 2 = 2·(−2) + (2g + 2)·1 = 2g − 2  ✓`);
    lines.push(`branch points: 2g + 2 = ${b}`);
    if(selected >= 0){
      lines.push('');
      lines.push(`branch point #${selected+1} clicked`);
      lines.push(`above: 1 ramification point; σ fixes it`);
      lines.push(`Weierstrass point of C (gap sequence ${guessWeierstrass(g)})`);
    } else {
      lines.push('');
      lines.push('click a branch point to inspect the involution');
    }
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }

  function guessWeierstrass(g){
    const gaps = [];
    for(let i=1;i<=g;i++) gaps.push(2*i - 1);
    return '{' + gaps.join(', ') + '}';
  }

  gEl.addEventListener('input', ()=>{ selected=-1; draw(); });
  svg.addEventListener('click', e=>{
    const t = e.target;
    if(t && t.tagName==='circle' && t.dataset.idx!==undefined){
      selected = +t.dataset.idx;
      draw();
    }
  });
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Each branch point of the cover is a Weierstrass point of $C$: a point where the gap sequence of $|np|$ deviates from the generic $\{1, 2, \ldots, g\}$. On a hyperelliptic curve the gap sequence at any branch point is $\{1, 3, 5, \ldots, 2g-1\}$, the maximum-jump pattern allowed. Generic curves have only finitely many Weierstrass points (with multiplicity, the count is $g^3 - g$); hyperelliptic curves concentrate them at the $2g+2$ ramification points.

See also
  • Branched covers and ramification
  • $g = 1$ as the "hyperelliptic" boundary case ($y^2 = $ cubic, $b = 4$)

6. Moduli of higher-genus curves $\mathcal{M}_g$

$\mathcal{M}_g$ is the moduli space (or rather, the moduli stack — Deligne–Mumford for $g \ge 2$) parametrising smooth projective curves of genus $g$ up to isomorphism. For $g \ge 2$ it has dimension $3g - 3$ as an algebraic stack over any base; over $\mathbb{C}$ it is a complex orbifold of complex dimension $3g - 3$ and $\mathrm{Teich}_g \to \mathcal{M}_g$ is its universal cover by Teichmüller space.

The dimension count is the deformation-theoretic statement $\dim T_C \mathcal{M}_g = h^1(C, T_C) = 3g - 3$ for $g \ge 2$. Three little tables of low-genus dimensions and structure:

genus $g$$\dim \mathcal{M}_g$hyperelliptic locus codimshape
$0$$0$n/a$\mathcal{M}_0 = \mathrm{pt}$
$1$$1$n/a$\mathcal{M}_{1,1} \cong \mathbb{A}^1_j$
$2$$3$$0$ — all curves are hyperelliptic$\mathcal{M}_2 = \mathcal{H}_2$, $3$-dim'l
$3$$6$$1$ — divisor $\mathcal{H}_3 \subset \mathcal{M}_3$generic = plane quartic
$g \ge 2$$3g - 3$$g - 2$open in $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$

The Deligne–Mumford compactification $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ adjoins stable nodal curves at the boundary: connected genus-$g$ curves with at worst nodal singularities and finite automorphism group. The boundary $\partial\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g = \overline{\mathcal{M}}_g \setminus \mathcal{M}_g$ decomposes as a union of boundary divisors $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_{\lfloor g/2\rfloor}$:

  • $\Delta_0$: irreducible curves with one self-node (pinching a non-separating cycle in $C$ — geometric genus drops by $1$).
  • $\Delta_i$ for $1 \le i \le \lfloor g/2 \rfloor$: nodal unions $C_1 \cup_p C_2$ with $g(C_1) = i$, $g(C_2) = g - i$ (pinching a separating cycle).

$\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ and its boundary divisors
click a node — see what nodal curve it parametrises
g = 4 → ⌊g/2⌋ + 1 = 3 boundary divisors
params.bodyScript:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = $('#svg-mg-boundary');
  const out = $('#out-mg-boundary');
  const gEl = $('#mg-g'), gValEl = $('#mg-g-val');
  let selected = null;

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    const g = +gEl.value;
    const half = Math.floor(g/2);
    gValEl.textContent = `g = ${g} → ⌊g/2⌋ + 1 = ${half+1} boundary divisors`;

    // central node: smooth Mg
    const cx = 270, cy = 150;
    drawNode(svg, cx, cy, 'M_g', {r:30, stroke:'var(--violet)', fill:'var(--panel2)', textColor:'var(--violet)', fontSize:13});
    const sub = SVG('text',{x:cx,y:cy+20,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--mute)'});
    sub.textContent = `dim ${3*g-3}`;
    svg.appendChild(sub);

    // boundary nodes around: Δ_0, Δ_1, ..., Δ_{half}
    const nodes = [];
    nodes.push({key:'D0', label:'Δ₀', cx:120, cy:60, color:'var(--pink)', sub:'irreducible nodal'});
    for(let i=1;i<=half;i++){
      // place around center
      const angle = -Math.PI/2 + (i / (half+0.4)) * Math.PI;
      const r = 140;
      const x = cx + r*Math.cos(angle);
      const y = cy + r*Math.sin(angle);
      nodes.push({key:`D${i}`, label:`Δ_${i}`, cx:x, cy:y, color:i===selected?'var(--cyan)':'var(--blue)', sub:`(${i}, ${g-i})`});
    }
    if(selected==='D0') nodes[0].color = 'var(--cyan)';

    // edges: smooth → each boundary divisor (closure relation)
    nodes.forEach(n=>{
      drawArrow(svg, [cx,cy], [n.cx,n.cy], {color:'var(--mute)', markerId:'mg-arr', dash:'3 3', curve:6});
    });

    // draw nodes and tag with click handlers
    nodes.forEach(n=>{
      const grp = SVG('g',{});
      const c = SVG('circle',{cx:n.cx,cy:n.cy,r:24,fill:'var(--panel2)','stroke':n.color,'stroke-width':2});
      c.style.cursor='pointer';
      c.dataset.key = n.key;
      grp.appendChild(c);
      const lab = SVG('text',{x:n.cx,y:n.cy+4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':13,fill:n.color,'font-weight':600});
      lab.textContent = n.label;
      lab.style.pointerEvents='none';
      grp.appendChild(lab);
      const t2 = SVG('text',{x:n.cx,y:n.cy+38,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t2.textContent = n.sub;
      t2.style.pointerEvents='none';
      grp.appendChild(t2);
      svg.appendChild(grp);
    });

    // small inset diagrams for the selected boundary class
    let lines = [];
    lines.push(`smooth Mg dim = 3g − 3 = ${3*g-3}`);
    lines.push(`Mg-bar = Mg ∪ ⋃_{i=0..⌊g/2⌋} Δ_i`);
    if(selected==='D0'){
      lines.push('');
      lines.push('Δ₀: pinch a non-separating loop on C');
      lines.push(`→ irreducible curve of geometric genus ${g-1} with one node`);
      lines.push('arithmetic genus stays at g (conductor formula).');
      // sketch in lower-left
      const ix = 380, iy = 250;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:`M ${ix} ${iy} q 30 -30 60 0 q 30 30 0 0 q -30 30 -60 0 q -30 -30 0 0 z`,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.6}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix+30,cy:iy,r:3,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix+30,y:iy+24,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--pink)'})).textContent='1 self-node';
    } else if(selected && selected.startsWith('D')){
      const i = +selected.slice(1);
      lines.push('');
      lines.push(`Δ_${i}: pinch a separating curve splitting C into`);
      lines.push(`(C₁, C₂) of genera (${i}, ${g-i})`);
      lines.push(`dim Δ_${i} = (3i−3) + (3(g−i)−3) + 1 (the node) = 3g − 5 (codim 1 in Mg-bar)`);
      const ix = 360, iy = 245;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix,cy:iy,r:18,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.6}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix,y:iy+4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--blue)'})).textContent=`g=${i}`;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix+50,cy:iy,r:18,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.6}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix+50,y:iy+4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--blue)'})).textContent=`g=${g-i}`;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix+25,cy:iy,r:3,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix+25,y:iy+24,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--pink)'})).textContent='1 node';
    } else {
      lines.push('');
      lines.push('click a Δ_i to see the corresponding nodal curve');
    }
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }

  gEl.addEventListener('input', ()=>{ selected=null; draw(); });
  svg.addEventListener('click', e=>{
    const t = e.target;
    if(t && t.tagName==='circle' && t.dataset.key){
      selected = t.dataset.key;
      draw();
    }
  });
  draw();
})();
</script>

      t2.textContent = n.sub;
      t2.style.pointerEvents='none';
      grp.appendChild(t2);
      svg.appendChild(grp);
    });

    // small inset diagrams for the selected boundary class
    let lines = [];
    lines.push(`smooth Mg dim = 3g − 3 = ${3*g-3}`);
    lines.push(`Mg-bar = Mg ∪ ⋃_{i=0..⌊g/2⌋} Δ_i`);
    if(selected==='D0'){
      lines.push('');
      lines.push('Δ₀: pinch a non-separating loop on C');
      lines.push(`→ irreducible curve of geometric genus ${g-1} with one node`);
      lines.push('arithmetic genus stays at g (conductor formula).');
      // sketch in lower-left
      const ix = 380, iy = 250;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:`M ${ix} ${iy} q 30 -30 60 0 q 30 30 0 0 q -30 30 -60 0 q -30 -30 0 0 z`,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--pink)','stroke-width':1.6}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix+30,cy:iy,r:3,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix+30,y:iy+24,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--pink)'})).textContent='1 self-node';
    } else if(selected && selected.startsWith('D')){
      const i = +selected.slice(1);
      lines.push('');
      lines.push(`Δ_${i}: pinch a separating curve splitting C into`);
      lines.push(`(C₁, C₂) of genera (${i}, ${g-i})`);
      lines.push(`dim Δ_${i} = (3i−3) + (3(g−i)−3) + 1 (the node) = 3g − 5 (codim 1 in Mg-bar)`);
      const ix = 360, iy = 245;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix,cy:iy,r:18,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.6}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix,y:iy+4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--blue)'})).textContent=`g=${i}`;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix+50,cy:iy,r:18,fill:'none',stroke:'var(--blue)','stroke-width':1.6}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix+50,y:iy+4,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--blue)'})).textContent=`g=${g-i}`;
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:ix+25,cy:iy,r:3,fill:'var(--pink)'}));
      svg.appendChild(SVG('text',{x:ix+25,y:iy+24,'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':10,fill:'var(--pink)'})).textContent='1 node';
    } else {
      lines.push('');
      lines.push('click a Δ_i to see the corresponding nodal curve');
    }
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }

  gEl.addEventListener('input', ()=>{ selected=null; draw(); });
  svg.addEventListener('click', e=>{
    const t = e.target;
    if(t && t.tagName==='circle' && t.dataset.key){
      selected = t.dataset.key;
      draw();
    }
  });
  draw();
})();
</script>

blocks.html: Each boundary divisor has codimension $1$ in $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$, so $\dim \Delta_i = 3g - 4$. The combinatorics of how boundary strata intersect — pinching $k$ disjoint cycles produces a stratum of codimension $k$, parametrised by a stable graph — make $\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g$ a textbook example of a stratified Deligne–Mumford stack. The boundary classes $\delta_0, \delta_1, \ldots, \delta_{\lfloor g/2 \rfloor} \in \mathrm{Pic}(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_g)$ together with the Hodge class $\lambda$ generate a finite-rank piece of the Picard group; the relations between them (Mumford, Cornalba–Harris) are the engine of intersection theory on the moduli of curves.
